text: Hello, my name is Dr. Eric Smith. I'm going to share with you my technique for robotic room wide gastric bypass.
I use a technique called camera hopping where I move the camera left and right and you'll see that indicated on the slides upcoming.
This patient had a hiatal hernia that was repaired at the beginning of the procedure and that will be included in another video.
The camera is positioned to the left, providing a view of the surgical field during the gastric bypass procedure.
Four trocars are positioned in a straight line above the umbilicus as the surgeon initiates a perigastric technique.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to dissect into the retrogastric space, with the camera positioned on the left-hand side providing a clear view of the area.
The surgeon moves the camera to the right and introduces the SureForm stapler with a blue load to begin creating the gastric pouch.
The surgeon angles the instrument upwards towards the spleen to create the initial spire of the gastric pouch.
The surgeon dissects approximately five centimeters below the gastroesophageal junction, targeting the area just beneath the first gastric vessel on the lesser curvature of the stomach.
The surgeon uses the tip of the instrument as a dissector to access the space towards the angle of His, maneuvering carefully to separate tissues in this area.

The surgeon notes that the angle of His has already been dissected due to a hiatal hernia. Typically, a vessel sealer is used to take down the peritoneal reflection at the angle of His to facilitate dissection and stapling.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the remaining attachments in the phrenogastric vessel area to finalize the creation of the gastric pouch.
A 36 French oral gastric tube is placed in the gastric pouch to calibrate and ensure the pouch is narrow.
The surgeon performs the final stapling just lateral to the gastroesophageal junction, creating a small, long cylindrical gastric pouch resembling a portion of an upper sleeve.
The surgeon repositions the camera to the left and uses a vessel sealer to dissect small vessels on the posterior aspect of the gastric pouch, preparing the area for eventual anastomosis.
The surgeon performs the anastomosis posterior to the staple line using cautery or a vessel sealer, with visible precision in the placement and sealing of tissue.
The surgeon identifies small vessels that do not significantly contribute to the formation of the gastric pouch, ensuring the pouch remains narrow and properly calibrated.
The surgeon grasps the omentum and elevates it, with the patient positioned in a slight reverse Trendelenburg, flexed approximately fifteen degrees at the upper torso.

The surgeon adjusts the patient's position to flatten the upper abdomen, facilitating the delivery of the omentum and preventing it from falling down. The slight reverse Trendelenburg position enhances visualization of the upper abdomen and upper foregut structures.
The surgeon gains access to the omentum located anterior to the transverse colon and divides it, then proceeds to locate the ligament of Treitz.
The surgeon runs the bowel approximately fifty centimeters in a clockwise fashion, preparing to suture it to the lateral aspect of the gastric pouch using a six-inch, two-oh absorbable V-lock suture.
The surgeon begins suturing laterally and runs posteriorly on the gastric pouch, securing the omega loop to the pouch as the first of four layers for a double-layer hand-sewn anastomosis.

The surgeon continues to run the suture, completing the first posterior layer of the anastomosis. The needle is laid over to the left for orientation, occasionally placed in a small amount of fat to prevent movement.
But this will, as I mentioned before, be the first row of the two-layer closure.
The surgeon introduces an oral gastric tube to provide internal pressure, then creates a gastrotomy using scissors and cautery.
The surgeon measures and adjusts the opening of the gastric pouch to ensure it is approximately twenty-five to thirty millimeters in size, preparing for the next step in the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon controls visible bleeding in the surgical field and proceeds to make an enterotomy on the jejunum, positioned below the previously created opening.
The fourth robotic arm applies traction and tension to the tissue, allowing the surgeon to avoid thermal injuries to the posterior aspect during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a six-inch absorbable V-lock suture to complete the full thickness posterior row of the gastric bypass anastomosis.
The surgeon begins suturing at the corner of the opening to facilitate placement of the anterior row, ensuring proximity to the starting point of the suture line.
The surgeon runs a full thickness suture across the posterior row of the gastric bypass site, ensuring the suture line is evenly placed along the tissue.
The surgeon brings the stitch out at the corner of the opening, threading it from inside to outside, preparing to tie it to the anterior full thickness layer.
The surgeon places one to two throws with the suture on the anterior full thickness layer, ensuring visibility before introducing the oral gastric tube.
The surgeon guides an oral gastric tube down into the Roux limb, which splays the tissue and ensures the anastomosis is not strictured or backwalled.
The surgeon uses a six-inch absorbable V-lock suture to run across the full thickness of the tissue, ensuring secure closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon ties the suture to the full thickness layer that was previously placed posteriorly, completing the connection between the anterior and posterior layers.
The surgeon places two or three suture throws to secure the anterior full thickness layer, ensuring the suture remains stable and does not back up.
The surgeon places the final suture, a six-inch absorbable V-lock, in a seromuscular fashion to imbricate the anterior suture line, completing the two-layered, hand-sewn gastrojejunostomy.
The surgeon incorporates the staple line into the tissue, rolling it in based on the available tissue. The staple line is tied to the initial seromuscular stitch placed posteriorly.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to create a small opening in the mesentery, then fires a white load to complete the anastomosis.
The surgeon completes the gastrojejunostomy and prepares to perform a leak test by occluding the anastomosis with the tip of an instrument.

The surgeon retracts the oral gastric tube slightly, ensuring it remains distal to the anastomosis. Openings are visible above and below the anastomosis site. Five cc's of indocyanine green (ICG) mixed in one hundred cc's of saline is injected to check for any extravasation.
The surgeon measures 150 centimeters from the gastrojejunostomy along the jejunum to identify the site for the upcoming jejunojejunostomy.
The surgeon places a 2-0 Vicryl suture to approximate the two limbs of the jejunum. Scissors with cautery are then used to create two small enterotomies in preparation for the jejunojejunostomy.
The camera is repositioned to the right, providing a clear view as the surgeon uses a stapler with a white load to perform the jejuno-jejunostomy, joining the two sections of the jejunum.
The surgeon inspects the staple line for any signs of bleeding. A nine-inch, two-oh absorbable V-lock suture is used to close the jejunal opening.
The surgeon uses a single suture to perform a two-layer closure of the jejunal opening, running it full thickness down and then back in a seromuscular fashion.
No matter which direction you close this, since it's hand sewn, I do not have to worry about any stricturing.
The surgeon uses a nine-inch, two-oh absorbable V-lock suture to perform a two-layer closure of the jejunal opening, ensuring sufficient length for the procedure.

The surgeon places an interrupted Vicryl suture at the tail of the previous suture to relieve tension from the barbs. The same Vicryl is then used to perform a Brolin stitch, similar to techniques used in laparoscopic surgeries.
The surgeon closes the mesenteric defect using a 2-0 Ethibond suture, applying a purse-string technique to secure the closure.
The surgeon performs a small slip knot to cinch down the suture, followed by the application of Arista on the staple line to aid in hemostasis.
The surgeon completes the Roux-en-Y gastric bypass procedure, with the final view showing the newly constructed gastric pouch and bypassed intestinal segments.
